panel.origin: The first Chief Justice of the United States (1789–1795), a Founding Father who helped shape the new federal judiciary.
panel.mixology: Jay led a Court with little defined power, and resigned to become governor of New York, then seen as a more important post.
panel.examples: A principal author of the Federalist Papers, he later negotiated the Jay Treaty with Britain, a controversial cornerstone of early foreign policy.

panel.origin: The fourth and longest-serving Chief Justice (1801–1835), regarded as the most important in the Court's history.
panel.mixology: Marshall's ruling in Marbury v. Madison established judicial review, the Court's power to strike down unconstitutional laws.
panel.examples: Over 34 years he transformed the Supreme Court from a weak body into a coequal branch of government, defining its authority for all time.

panel.origin: The fifth Chief Justice (1836–1864), whose tenure is forever defined by one catastrophic decision.
panel.mixology: Taney authored the Dred Scott decision, ruling that Black Americans could not be citizens, a ruling that helped precipitate the Civil War.
panel.examples: Though he had a long career and earlier reputation for ability, that single 1857 opinion stands as one of the most reviled in American legal history.

panel.origin: The sixth Chief Justice (1864–1873), who presided over the nation during Reconstruction.
panel.mixology: Chase presided over the impeachment trial of President Andrew Johnson, lending the proceedings judicial gravity.
panel.examples: A former Treasury secretary and abolitionist, he had ambitions for the presidency himself even while leading the Court.

panel.origin: The eighth Chief Justice (1888–1910), who led the Court through the Gilded Age.
panel.mixology: Fuller's Court issued rulings that limited federal economic regulation, reflecting the era's laissez-faire philosophy.
panel.examples: His long tenure spanned a period of rapid industrialization, with a Court often siding with business against reform efforts.

panel.origin: The tenth Chief Justice (1921–1930) and the only person to lead both the executive and judicial branches.
panel.mixology: Taft is the only person to serve as both President of the United States and Chief Justice, a unique distinction.
panel.examples: He considered the Chief Justiceship the greater honor, and successfully lobbied for the Court's own building, separating it from Congress.

panel.origin: The 11th Chief Justice (1930–1941), who steered the Court through the constitutional crisis of the New Deal.
panel.mixology: Hughes navigated President Roosevelt's court-packing threat, presiding as the Court shifted to uphold New Deal legislation.
panel.examples: He had earlier resigned from the Court to run for president, narrowly losing in 1916, before returning years later to lead it.

panel.origin: The 14th Chief Justice (1953–1969), who led one of the most transformative and activist eras in the Court's history.
panel.mixology: Warren's Court issued Brown v. Board of Education, ending legal school segregation, and expanded civil liberties dramatically.
panel.examples: A former California governor, his tenure reshaped American law on civil rights, criminal procedure, and the principle of one person, one vote.

panel.origin: The 15th Chief Justice (1969–1986), who led a Court that was more moderate than expected.
panel.mixology: Burger wrote the unanimous opinion in United States v. Nixon, forcing the president to release the Watergate tapes.
panel.examples: Though appointed to rein in the liberal Warren Court, his tenure preserved many of its precedents while addressing new constitutional questions.

panel.origin: The 16th Chief Justice (1986–2005), who guided the Court in a more conservative direction over nearly two decades.
panel.mixology: Rehnquist championed federalism and states' rights, and presided over the impeachment trial of President Bill Clinton.
panel.examples: Elevated from associate justice, his long leadership marked a deliberate shift away from the expansive rulings of the Warren era.

panel.origin: The 17th and current Chief Justice (2005–present), known for his concern with the Court's institutional legitimacy.
panel.mixology: Roberts cast a decisive vote to uphold the Affordable Care Act, surprising many and reflecting his institutionalist instincts.
panel.examples: He famously likened a judge's role to a baseball umpire calling balls and strikes, a metaphor for judicial restraint that has defined his public image.

panel.origin: An associate justice (1902–1932) often called one of the greatest legal minds in American history.
panel.mixology: Holmes's incisive opinions and dissents, prizing judicial restraint and free speech, profoundly shaped American law.
panel.examples: Known as 'The Great Dissenter,' his eloquent minority opinions frequently became the law of the land generations later.

panel.origin: An associate justice (1916–1939), the first Jewish member of the Court and a champion of individual rights.
panel.mixology: Brandeis pioneered the use of real-world social and economic data in legal arguments, the famous 'Brandeis brief.'
panel.examples: A fierce critic of monopoly and defender of privacy, his appointment was bitterly contested but proved enormously influential.

panel.origin: An associate justice (1937–1971) known for his absolutist defense of the Bill of Rights.
panel.mixology: Black argued the First Amendment's protections were absolute, reading 'no law' abridging free speech to mean exactly that.
panel.examples: A former senator, he was a leading force in applying the Bill of Rights to the states, expanding individual liberties nationwide.

panel.origin: An associate justice (1939–1962) and leading advocate of judicial restraint.
panel.mixology: Frankfurter believed courts should defer to elected legislatures, often clashing with colleagues who favored bolder intervention.
panel.examples: An immigrant and former Harvard Law professor, he was a towering intellectual whose philosophy of restraint influenced generations of jurists.

panel.origin: The longest-serving justice in the Court's history (1939–1975), a fierce defender of civil liberties and the environment.
panel.mixology: Douglas served over 36 years and authored the opinion finding a constitutional right to privacy in Griswold v. Connecticut.
panel.examples: A passionate individualist and outdoorsman, his record-setting tenure and bold libertarian streak made him one of the Court's most colorful figures.

panel.origin: An associate justice (1956–1990) and one of the most influential liberal voices of the modern Court.
panel.mixology: Brennan was a master coalition-builder whose opinions shaped law on free speech, civil rights, and criminal justice.
panel.examples: Appointed by a Republican president, he became a leading architect of the Warren Court's revolution, famously counting votes on his fingers — 'five.'

panel.origin: The first Black Supreme Court Justice (1967–1991), and the lawyer who had argued and won Brown v. Board of Education.
panel.mixology: Before joining the Court, Marshall won the landmark case ending school segregation as the nation's leading civil rights attorney.
panel.examples: His appointment broke a centuries-old color barrier, and on the bench he remained a passionate champion of equality and individual rights.

panel.origin: The first woman to serve on the Supreme Court (1981–2006), often the decisive swing vote in closely divided cases.
panel.mixology: O'Connor's pragmatic, case-by-case approach made her the pivotal vote on issues from abortion to affirmative action for years.
panel.examples: Her appointment shattered the Court's all-male history, and her centrist influence made her, for a time, arguably the most powerful jurist in America.

panel.origin: An associate justice (1993–2020), a pioneering advocate for gender equality who became a cultural icon.
panel.mixology: Before the Court, Ginsburg argued landmark gender-discrimination cases; on it, she became a powerful, pointed dissenter.
panel.examples: Affectionately nicknamed 'The Notorious R.B.G.,' her fierce dissents and lifelong fight for equal rights made her a beloved public figure.

panel.origin: The first Latina and first Hispanic Supreme Court Justice (2009–present).
panel.mixology: Sotomayor's appointment marked a historic milestone, and she has become a leading liberal voice known for her pointed dissents.
panel.examples: Raised in a Bronx housing project, her path from humble beginnings to the nation's highest court embodies a powerful American story.

panel.origin: The first Black woman to serve on the Supreme Court (2022–present).
panel.mixology: Jackson's confirmation broke a historic barrier, adding the perspective of a former public defender to the bench.
panel.examples: A graduate of Harvard and a former federal judge, her appointment continued the slow diversification of an institution long dominated by white men.

panel.origin: An associate justice (1991–present) and a leading conservative voice, known for his originalist philosophy.
panel.mixology: Thomas advocates interpreting the Constitution by its original public meaning, and is often the Court's most willing to revisit precedent.
panel.examples: Only the second Black justice, his contentious confirmation and his long, influential conservative tenure have made him a defining figure.

panel.origin: An associate justice (1986–2016) and the intellectual champion of originalism and textualism.
panel.mixology: Scalia argued laws and the Constitution should be read by their text and original meaning, transforming American legal interpretation.
panel.examples: His vivid, combative writing and forceful dissents made him perhaps the most influential conservative jurist of his generation.

panel.origin: An associate justice (1988–2018) who served for decades as the Court's pivotal swing vote.
panel.mixology: Kennedy authored the landmark opinion in Obergefell v. Hodges, establishing a nationwide right to same-sex marriage.
panel.examples: His unpredictable, deciding vote in major cases on liberty and equality gave him outsized influence over the law for a generation.

panel.origin: An associate justice (1994–2022) known for a pragmatic approach focused on a law's real-world consequences.
panel.mixology: Breyer argued for interpreting laws by their purpose and practical effects, a counterpoint to the originalism of his colleagues.
panel.examples: A consensus-seeker who valued the Court's collegiality, he stepped down after nearly three decades to allow a timely successor.

panel.origin: An associate justice (2006–present) and a reliably conservative voice on the Court.
panel.mixology: Alito authored the 2022 opinion in Dobbs that overturned Roe v. Wade, ending the constitutional right to abortion.
panel.examples: A former prosecutor and appeals judge, his consequential majority opinions have made him a central figure in the Court's conservative turn.

panel.origin: An associate justice (2010–present), a former Solicitor General and Harvard Law dean known for her sharp, accessible writing.
panel.mixology: Kagan is regarded as one of the Court's most effective writers, crafting clear, pointed opinions and dissents.
panel.examples: She was the first justice in decades appointed without prior experience as a judge, having instead led one of the nation's top law schools.

panel.origin: An associate justice (2017–present), an originalist in the mold of the seat's previous occupant.
panel.mixology: Gorsuch surprised observers by authoring a ruling extending federal civil rights protections to LGBTQ workers, on textualist grounds.
panel.examples: Known for his strong defense of textualism, his independent streak occasionally produces rulings that cross the Court's usual ideological lines.

panel.origin: An associate justice (2020–present), a former law professor and the Court's youngest current member at her appointment.
panel.mixology: Barrett, a self-described originalist, was a student and clerk of Antonin Scalia, whose interpretive philosophy she shares.
panel.examples: Her confirmation just before the 2020 election solidified a conservative majority, marking a significant moment in the Court's modern trajectory.

EARLY CHIEF JUSTICES.en: John Marshall's ruling in Marbury v. Madison established judicial review — the Court's power to strike down unconstitutional laws.

MODERN CHIEF JUSTICES.en: William Howard Taft is the only person in history to serve as both President and Chief Justice of the United States.

LANDMARK JUSTICES.en: Oliver Wendell Holmes Jr. was nicknamed 'The Great Dissenter' because his minority opinions so often became law generations later.

TRAILBLAZERS.en: Thurgood Marshall won Brown v. Board of Education as a lawyer before later becoming the first Black Supreme Court Justice.

CONTEMPORARY COURT.en: Anthony Kennedy served for decades as the pivotal swing vote, authoring the opinion that established nationwide same-sex marriage.
